summary: Pretty crazy day ITP with Reynoldstown finally seeing construction start on that corner project near the Beltline, while airport chaos continues with ICE agents walking around Hartsfield-Jackson as the TSA struggles with the shutdown. Meanwhile, a tragic shooting in Ellenwood took a 7-year-old's life.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Corner Project Near Beltline Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After nearly five years of sitting as an empty lot, that corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action. Embry Development is taking over from Toll Brothers to build 85 townhomes and condos about a block from the Eastside Trail. You know the spot - it's been cleared forever and neighbors have been wondering what was going to happen there.
topStory.body:
If you've walked the Eastside Trail lately, you've probably noticed construction equipment on that corner lot at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace that's been sitting empty since 2021. Well, it's finally happening. Atlanta-based Embry Development Company has taken over the project from national developer Toll Brothers and is moving forward with 85 residential units just a block west of the Beltline.

The 2-acre site has been a bit of an eyesore in Reynoldstown, especially given how hot the neighborhood has become with all the Beltline activity. Embry bought the property two years ago with plans to take over the already-permitted development, but construction has been slow to start. The project, now called Camber Crossing, will include both townhomes and for-sale condos.

For a neighborhood that's seen so much change thanks to the Beltline, this feels like another piece of the puzzle falling into place. The location is prime - you can practically roll out of bed and onto the trail. And honestly, after staring at that empty lot for years, most neighbors are probably just happy to see something happening there, even if it means more density in an area that's already seeing plenty of development pressure.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Reynoldstown development site on Chester Avenue

The 2-acre site has been a bit of an eyesore in Reynoldstown, especially given how hot the neighborhood has become with all the Beltline activity. Embry bought the property two years ago with plans to take over the already-permitted development, but construction has been slow to start. stories.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Reynoldstown development site on Chester Avenue

stories.headline: Decatur's 'Missing Middle' Housing Village Gets Ready to Launch
stories.summary: The Cottages at Midway is about to soft launch with 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards near downtown Decatur. It's designed as more accessible ITP housing where median home values have hit over $700,000. The pocket neighborhood on Midway Road is trying to fill that gap between apartments and million-dollar houses.
stories.body:
You know how everyone complains about the lack of affordable housing ITP? Well, here's one attempt to tackle that problem. The Cottages at Midway is getting ready to launch next week as a 'missing middle' community near Decatur, offering 18 standalone houses that won't completely break the bank. We're talking about 2883 and 2893 Midway Road, where two underused lots are being transformed into something more reasonable for normal humans.

The whole concept is built around shared courtyards and greenspace, which sounds pretty nice honestly. When you consider that median home values in Decatur proper have climbed past $700,000, this project is trying to bridge that impossible gap between tiny condos and houses that cost more than most people make in a decade. It's mixed-income, so not everything will be 'affordable' in the true sense, but at least it's an attempt.

The timing feels right too. People want to be ITP, they want to be near Decatur's walkable downtown and good schools, but they also want to, you know, not go bankrupt buying a house. Whether this model actually works and gets replicated elsewhere remains to be seen, but it's definitely worth watching as one potential solution to our housing crisis.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing the layout of Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: West End Townhomes Along Beltline Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: The GW Parkside project (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is finally rising up along the Beltline in West End. Ackerman & Co. is building 23 for-sale townhomes at 600 Hopkins St., with the first seven units almost ready and hitting the market this spring. It's relatively rare to see for-sale townhomes along the Beltline's southwest stretch.
stories.body:
Here's some good news for the West End - those townhomes along the Beltline that have been in the works forever are actually starting to look like real buildings. The project at 600 Hopkins Street, now called GW Parkside (much better than the old Hopkins and White Townhomes name), is being developed by Ackerman & Co., the same folks behind Lee + White and all its cool food and office spaces.

What makes this project interesting is that for-sale townhomes are pretty rare along the southwest stretch of the Beltline. Most of what we've seen has been apartments and condos, so having 23 actual townhomes is kind of a big deal. The first seven units are almost topped out and should hit the market this spring, which is perfect timing for people who want to be on the Beltline but also want a little more space and privacy.

The West End has been seeing some serious investment lately, and this feels like another step in the neighborhood's evolution. You're right there on the trail, close to all the action at Lee + White, and you get an actual house with your own front door. For a lot of people, that's going to be pretty appealing, especially if the pricing ends up being reasonable compared to what townhomes cost in other Beltline neighborhoods.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes under construction in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. The menu features soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa, Peking chicken for two ($25), and cocktails like Thai tea martinis. Both chefs have serious restaurant credentials and this feels like a solid addition to Inman Park's food scene.
stories.body:
There's a new spot in Inman Park that you should definitely check out. Mushi Ni opened yesterday on Elizabeth Street (you know, just off the Freedom Park Trail), and it's the first restaurant from chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez. These aren't just random people opening a restaurant - Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, so they know what they're doing.

The menu is all about Asian-American comfort food, and it sounds really good. We're talking soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, crab Rangoon puffs, and their signature Peking chicken platters that feed two people for just $25. They're also doing cocktails, including a Thai tea martini that honestly sounds amazing. The whole concept started as a pop-up, which usually means they've worked out the kinks.

What I love about this is the location - you can literally hop off the Freedom Park Trail and grab dinner. Inman Park has always had good food options, but having another quality Asian spot in the neighborhood just makes it that much better. Plus, if you're doing lunch and dinner service right from day one, that shows confidence. Can't wait to try that Peking chicken.
stories.imageAlt: Interior shot of Mushi Ni restaurant showing dining room setup

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style BBQ to the Beltline
stories.summary: John Lewis, the pitmaster behind Charleston's famous barbecue, has opened at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse. The smokehouse upstairs has panoramic windows overlooking the Beltline pedestrian bridge and runs six 22-foot smokers 24/7. This is some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta, with that Texas-style approach that made Lewis famous.
stories.body:
Okay, this is huge for barbecue lovers. John Lewis, who helped put Charleston on the BBQ map, has opened Lewis Barbecue at Ansley Mall, right behind The Cook's Warehouse and walking distance from the Beltline. But here's what makes this place special - the smokehouse itself. Most BBQ places hide their smokers in some back building, but Lewis put his upstairs in a loft with huge windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. It's like the fanciest engine room you've ever seen.

They're running six 22-foot smokers that Lewis helped weld himself from 1,000-gallon propane tanks. These things run 24 hours a day in staggered eight-hour shifts, which is serious commitment to the craft. And the brisket - this is Texas-style barbecue done right, with that perfect bark and smoke ring that's been missing from Atlanta's BBQ scene. Lewis knows what he's doing; he's been perfecting this since his Charleston days.

The location is perfect too. You can walk over from the Beltline, grab some of the best barbecue in the city, and watch the smokehouse operation through those big windows. It's dinner and a show, and honestly, Atlanta's barbecue scene just got a lot more interesting. This isn't your typical strip mall BBQ joint - this is the real deal from someone who's been doing it at the highest level for years.
stories.imageAlt: Barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Food Hall Opens in April
stories.summary: The founder of NYC's Gansevoort Market is opening Vela Park Food Works near East Lake Golf Club in mid-April. It's his third Atlanta food destination and will feature six chef-driven concepts plus craft cocktails and outdoor dining. This is part of the new Vela Park rental community that's been under construction.
stories.body:
Here's some exciting food news for the eastside. Robert Montwaid, the guy who created New York City's famous Gansevoort Market, is opening his third Atlanta food project in mid-April. Vela Park Food Works will be the centerpiece of the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club, and it sounds like it's going to be pretty special.

We're talking about six different chef-driven concepts all under one roof, plus craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining space. This isn't just another generic food court situation - Montwaid has a track record of curating really interesting food experiences, and his previous Atlanta projects have been solid. The timing is perfect too, with the weather getting nice and people wanting to eat outside again.

The location makes sense as well. East Lake has been seeing more development and foot traffic, and having a proper food hall in that area fills a real gap. Plus, if you're familiar with Montwaid's other work, you know he's good at bringing together different food concepts that actually complement each other rather than just throwing random restaurants together. Should be a nice addition to the neighborhood once it opens.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing dining area

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Project Nearly Finished on West Howard
stories.summary: That bike lane project on West Howard Avenue between downtown Decatur and Atlanta's city limits is almost done after being in the works for nearly a decade. The mile-long stretch now has a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures. It's a major transformation from the old drag strip setup.
stories.body:
Remember when West Howard Avenue was basically a drag strip for cars trying to speed between Decatur and Atlanta? Well, that's about to be ancient history. The Complete Streets project that's been in planning for nearly a decade is almost finished, and it's a pretty dramatic transformation. We're talking about a full mile from Commerce Drive (near Decatur High) all the way to the city limits at East Lake MARTA.

The big change is the two-way cycle track with concrete barriers that runs most of the length. This isn't just painted bike lanes that cars ignore - these are physically separated, which makes a huge difference for actually feeling safe on a bike. They've also added proper sidewalks and traffic calming measures to slow down cars. It's the kind of infrastructure that makes you think maybe we can actually make streets work for people, not just cars.

What's cool is how this connects to the bigger transportation network. You can bike from downtown Decatur all the way to East Lake MARTA now without taking your life in your hands. And honestly, West Howard needed this badly - it was always this weird barrier between neighborhoods where pedestrians and cyclists were basically afterthoughts. Now it feels like an actual street that people might want to spend time on rather than just speed through.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lane on West Howard Avenue in Decatur with concrete barriers

stories.headline: ICE Agents Walk Around Hartsfield-Jackson as Airport Chaos Continues
stories.summary: Federal immigration officers are patrolling Atlanta's airport to help with security as TSA agents call out sick during the shutdown. The airport is telling people to arrive four hours early for flights, and Monday saw the highest rate of TSA no-shows since the shutdown began. The agents aren't doing immigration enforcement, just providing security presence.
stories.body:
The situation at Hartsfield-Jackson is getting pretty surreal. Armed ICE agents in tactical gear are now walking around the airport as the partial government shutdown creates massive TSA staffing shortages. Nearly 3,500 TSA agents across the country called out Monday, and Atlanta saw some of the worst delays with the airport recommending four-hour early arrivals. That's not a typo - four hours.

The ICE agents aren't actually doing immigration enforcement or security screenings, which honestly makes their presence feel more like political theater than practical help. They're just there to provide 'security presence' while TSA workers who haven't been paid in weeks continue to quit or call in sick. One traveler spent 12 hours trying to get through the airport system, which is absolutely insane for what's supposed to be the world's busiest and most efficient airport.

What's really frustrating is that this was entirely predictable and avoidable. When you don't pay people for weeks, they stop showing up to work - it's not rocket science. But instead of fixing the underlying problem (ending the shutdown and paying people), we get this weird militarized response that doesn't actually solve anything. Meanwhile, regular travelers are stuck dealing with the chaos while politicians play games with people's livelihoods.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents in tactical gear at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: 7-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in Ellenwood
stories.summary: A tragic accident took the life of a 7-year-old boy overnight on Loveless Drive in DeKalb County. Family members say the child was playing with a gun when it went off. Police are treating it as an unintended discharge and investigating the circumstances around how the child got access to the firearm.
stories.body:
This is just heartbreaking. A 7-year-old boy died overnight in what appears to be an accidental shooting at a home on Loveless Drive in Ellenwood. DeKalb County police found the child dead inside the residence during the early morning hours, and family members told reporters the boy was playing with a gun when it discharged. Police are calling it a 'non-intended discharge of a weapon' and investigating how this happened.

The tragedy drew a large crowd of grieving family and friends to the neighborhood, and you can only imagine what they're going through right now. The road was blocked for hours while investigators worked the scene, trying to piece together exactly what led to this preventable loss of life. These kinds of accidents happen way too often, and it's always children who pay the price.

This is exactly why gun safety advocates talk so much about secure storage. Kids are naturally curious, and if there's a gun in the house, they will find it. Trigger locks, gun safes, separating ammunition - these aren't just suggestions, they're literally life and death measures. One family in Ellenwood is learning that the hard way today, and no family should have to go through this kind of tragedy.
stories.imageAlt: Police vehicles outside residence on Loveless Drive where child shooting occurred

stories.headline: Man Killed Outside Vine City Market
stories.summary: A man died after being shot Tuesday night outside a market on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard in the Vine City neighborhood. Atlanta police haven't released details about what led to the shooting or any suspect information. It happened just before 9 p.m. in the 900 block, and the victim was rushed to a hospital where he died.
stories.body:
Another deadly shooting in Atlanta, this time in Vine City. A man was shot and killed Tuesday night outside a neighborhood market on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard, just east of Joseph E. Lowery Boulevard. Atlanta police responded just before 9 p.m. and found the victim with gunshot wounds. He was rushed to the hospital but didn't make it.

Police haven't released much information yet about what happened or whether they have any suspects. It's unclear if this was a robbery, a personal dispute, or something else entirely. The victim's identity hasn't been released either, so families are probably waiting for word about whether their loved one is coming home tonight.

This shooting happened around the same time as another incident less than a mile away on Martin Luther King Jr. Drive, where someone was shot and a child was injured by debris. APD hasn't said if the shootings are connected, but having two violent incidents so close together in such a short time frame is concerning for the neighborhood. Vine City has been working hard on revitalization efforts, and this kind of violence doesn't help those efforts at all.
stories.imageAlt: Police scene at Vine City market where shooting occurred

stories.headline: Person Shot, Child Hurt by Debris on MLK Drive
stories.summary: A shooting on Martin Luther King Jr. Drive SW left one person hospitalized and a child injured by debris Tuesday evening. The shooting happened around 8:30 p.m. in the 800 block, and you can see where bullets went through the windows of a nearby barber shop. Police haven't identified suspects or explained what led to the gunfire.
stories.body:
There was another shooting in southwest Atlanta Tuesday night, this one on Martin Luther King Jr. Drive that left one person shot and a child injured by debris. It happened around 8:30 p.m. in the 800 block, and the scene shows just how chaotic it must have been. The windows of a nearby barber shop called 'Hairkut' were completely shot out, with glass scattered across the sidewalk.

The person who was shot drove themselves to the hospital and is listed as stable, which is good news. But the fact that a child was injured by debris shows how these shootings affect more than just the intended targets. A barber who was cutting hair when the shooting started described hearing the gunshots while he was working with a client. That's terrifying - just trying to do your job and suddenly bullets are flying.

Atlanta police haven't released information about suspects or what caused the shooting. It happened less than a mile from another deadly shooting in Vine City around the same time, though police haven't said if they're connected. Either way, it's been a violent night in that part of the city, and residents deserve to feel safe in their own neighborhoods. The fact that a barber shop got shot up while people were just trying to get haircuts really drives home how random and dangerous this kind of violence can be.
stories.imageAlt: Damaged storefront with broken windows from shooting on MLK Drive

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls While Fire Union Contract Festers
stories.summary: Atlanta City Council's Public Safety Committee unanimously passed two resolutions targeting ICE operations in the city - one banning ICE detention facilities and another requiring APD to document when ICE agents are present during police operations. Meanwhile, dozens of people showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' union contract that council already approved.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council is taking a stand against ICE operations while Mayor Dickens is catching heat for his handling of the fire department union contract. The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced two resolutions - one that would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits and another requiring Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations. The full council will vote on both on April 20.

But the bigger drama was dozens of Atlantans showing up to blast Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever contract with the firefighters' union. Council ratified this contract back in April 2025, but the mayor has been sitting on it ever since. This is pretty unprecedented - when council approves something like this, the mayor usually signs it or explains why he won't.

The timing is particularly awkward given that ICE agents are currently walking around Hartsfield-Jackson as part of the federal shutdown response. Here's council trying to limit ICE activities in Atlanta while federal agents are already operating at our airport. As for the fire contract, this standoff between the mayor and council is exactly the kind of political dysfunction that drives people crazy about local government. Either sign it or explain why you won't, but don't just ignore it.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with community members in attendance

stories.headline: DeKalb Parents Rage Against School Closure Plans
stories.summary: Hundreds of frustrated parents packed Lakeside High on March 23 for another DeKalb Schools input session on potential closures. Parents and kids from Oak Grove and Evansdale elementary even protested outside beforehand, calling the whole Student Assignment Project a 'farce.' The district is holding more sessions this week but parents feel like they're not being heard.
stories.body:
DeKalb County Schools is really stepping in it with these school closure proposals. Hundreds of angry parents showed up at Lakeside High for another public input session, and parents from Oak Grove and Evansdale elementary schools were outside protesting with their kids before the meeting even started. They're calling the whole Student Assignment Project a 'farce' and saying the district isn't actually listening to community input.

This is the revised list of potential closures after the district got massive pushback on their first proposal. But parents aren't buying that this is a real process of gathering input. One Oak Grove parent told Rough Draft Atlanta, 'They are giving us lip service, they're saying they're listening to us, it's a process. This is not a process. This is a farce, and they're not listening.' When you have parents bringing their elementary school kids to protests, you know you've lost the room.

The district has six more input sessions scheduled this week, but the damage to trust seems pretty severe. School closures are always controversial, but the way DeKalb is handling this feels particularly tone-deaf. Parents invest in neighborhoods partly because of school assignments, and when you threaten to upend that without meaningful community input, people get rightfully pissed. The district needs to either genuinely listen to these concerns or be honest about what decisions have already been made.
stories.imageAlt: Parents and children protesting DeKalb school closure plans outside meeting

stories.headline: No Kings Protests Coming to Decatur This Saturday
stories.summary: Thousands of 'No Kings' protests are planned nationwide on Saturday, March 28, including events in Decatur and Tucker. These are protests against President Trump's actions since starting his second term. Part of a broader national movement with demonstrations happening in communities across the country.
stories.body:
Get ready for some political activity this weekend. Decatur and Tucker are both hosting 'No Kings' protests on Saturday, March 28, as part of thousands of demonstrations planned nationwide. These protests are specifically targeting President Trump's actions since the beginning of his second term, and they're happening in communities large and small across the country.

The name 'No Kings' is a reference to the idea that America was founded in opposition to monarchy and concentrated executive power. Organizers are clearly drawing parallels between Trump's presidency and authoritarian governance, which is going to resonate with a lot of people in DeKalb County given how blue this area tends to vote. Expect decent turnout, especially in Decatur.

What's interesting is how decentralized this movement appears to be, with local organizers in different communities setting up their own events rather than one big centralized demonstration. That suggests there's real grassroots energy behind this rather than just top-down organizing. Whether these protests have any real political impact remains to be seen, but they're definitely a way for people who are unhappy with the current administration to come together and make their voices heard.
stories.imageAlt: Previous protest gathering showing demonstrators with signs
